text:
Coal-to-olefins has been said to reduce China's reliance on oil to keep the petchem industry running.

My very rudimentary way of explaining it:

The heart of the petrochemical industry is naphtha, which is produced from the CDU. You then use this to put into a steam cracker and produce ethylene, propylene and a bunch of other cracked products. These are all called "Olefins". These is then used into the downstream petchem derivatives industry to make all sorts of plastics.

There is another technology where you take COAL and make ethylene and propylene out of it. This is where the term Coal-to-olefins (CTO) is born. Essentially you bypass the steam cracker entirely. Presto! No oil needed to manufacture plastics!

What China then did with the freed up naphtha is to divert it into mogas blending instead to support the transportation demand. Tada! Oil demand reduced!

Not sure how structural this would be, but if the econs make sense and the government doesn't disapprove of CTO, why won't it be? China also brought online several steam crackers that takes ethane as feedstock. Yet another clever way to diversify out of naphtha.

Of course, the rest of Asia does not have this flexibility. So naphtha (and LPG) still would be required to keep the rest of their petchem units running.

Just to add a few thoughts—while I’m still cautious about calling a fundamental bottom in gold, Thursday’s NFP print forced the market to scale back its hyper-aggressive tightening bets.

We could certainly dissect the finer details of the NFP data, like the participation rate, but the tape tends to fixate on the headline payroll figure. The 2s10s curve had been on a relentless flattening path recently, but it shifted into a bull steepening regime right after the report dropped.

The market had tilted so sharply into the 'hawkish Warsh' narrative the short-rate space and the USD became heavily overcrowded. I suspect the upcoming data won't back up those extreme bets, so I’m anticipating a gradual unwinding.

As that unwind plays out, gold, PGMs, and potentially BTC could catch a significant bid. For gold to kick off a clean, beautiful rally, it really needs to be fueled by a USD rollover.

The absolute best case scenario would be the DXY breaking below 100 and building downside momentum. That said i’m fully aware of the risk that if easing US growth expectations coincide with a rollback of tightening bets from foreign central banks like the ECB, the greenback might not flush as hard as people think.

That’s exactly why I’m keeping my guard up. While it would be ideal if the market just rallies from here without giving the weak hands a chance to get back in, I’m holding onto a massive cash cushion to buffer against the risk of another flush.

Because I don't view this as a definitive floor, retail investors trying to play gold via leveraged ETFs are likely setting themselves up for a world of psychological and financial pain.

Frankly, anyone trading 3x leveraged gold or gold miners is out of their mind. In a space this vol, it blows right past what a retail account can realistically survive, meaning they'll likely get stopped out or capitulate long before we ever reach the final destination.

The right playbook here is either using derivatives to build a structured expression or slowly accumulating low-leverage cash equities. At the very least, the r/r ratio has cleared into an highly attractive zone.

AI Leads US Job Cuts for Record 4th Month as Tech Claims 31% of H1 Layoffs | Mark Rutherford, Techtimes

Artificial intelligence has become the leading stated reason for US job cuts for four consecutive months — a streak with no precedent in outplacement data — and the technology sector has absorbed nearly a third of all US layoff announcements in the first half of 2026, according to a report released Wednesday by outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas. Between January and June, US tech employers announced 139,156 job cuts — an 83% surge from the 76,214 recorded in the same period of 2025 — while AI was explicitly cited in 101,743 of all US layoff announcements this year, representing approximately 23% of every cut tracked by Challenger, Gray & Christmas across the entire economy.

The back-to-back-to-back-to-back months in which AI ranked as the leading stated reason for layoffs marks a structural shift that Challenger's own data — which began tracking AI as a distinct cut reason in 2023 — has not recorded before. "Tech remains the epicenter of this year's cuts," said Andy Challenger, chief revenue officer at the Chicago-based firm. "AI is the dominant force as companies are restructuring around it, automating roles, and reallocating budgets toward new capabilities. The sector is being reshaped in real time."

But the firms driving that streak are, in almost every case, the same firms posting their strongest-ever financial results — a paradox that has drawn escalating scrutiny from economists, labor researchers, and the workers on the receiving end of the cuts.

What Tech's 31% Share of US Layoffs Actually Means for Workers

The headline June numbers offered some surface-level relief. US employers across all sectors announced 45,849 job cuts during the month — the lowest monthly total since December 2025, down 53% from May's 97,006. Tech specifically saw 15,503 cuts in June, down sharply from May's 38,242, which had been the sector's highest single-month total since August 2024.

Challenger attributed the June moderation to seasonal patterns rather than any underlying change in direction. "The pace of layoffs cooled considerably in June, similar to plans last June, and as is typical for summer months," he said. "That said, the cuts we are seeing remain concentrated in technology, and artificial intelligence continues to reshape how companies think about headcount."

The full first-half picture reinforces that reading. Total US layoff announcements reached 443,604 through June — down 40% from the government-distorted 744,308 recorded in H1 2025, when mass federal workforce reductions drove that period's total to historic highs. Adjusting for that distortion, 2026 is running roughly in line with 2024. What has fundamentally changed is where the cuts are falling: tech's share has ballooned to nearly a third of the remaining total, a concentration last seen during the post-pandemic correction of 2022-2023. In the second quarter alone, employers announced 226,242 cuts, up 4% from Q1's 217,362 — signaling that the year's structural trend is not abating even as monthly figures bounce seasonally.

Profitable Companies Cut While Committing Record Capex

The Challenger data crystallizes what analysts have called the defining paradox of 2026's technology labor market: the same companies eliminating tens of thousands of roles are simultaneously committing to the largest capital investment cycle in the industry's history.

Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta have collectively guided 2026 capital expenditure to an estimated $700 billion — nearly double their combined 2025 actual spend — with the bulk directed at AI data centers, custom silicon, and inference infrastructure. Meta reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of $56.3 billion, up 33% year over year, then executed 8,000 layoffs in May while raising its 2026 capex guidance to $115-145 billion. Google Cloud revenue grew 63% year over year in Q1 2026. Microsoft reported revenue of $82.9 billion and operating income of $38.4 billion in its fiscal third quarter of 2026, up 18% and 20% year over year respectively.

The financial logic driving simultaneous cuts and capex is visible in corporate filings. Meta's $115-145 billion infrastructure spending envelope dwarfs its human payroll budget — meaning the layoffs function more as a financing mechanism than as a direct cost-saving exercise. Cisco provides the year's starkest illustration: on May 14, the San Jose networking company notified roughly 4,000 workers of their elimination on the same day it reported record quarterly revenue of $15.8 billion, a 12% year-on-year gain. CFO Mark Patterson told analysts that the restructuring was "not a savings-driven" exercise but a rapid reallocation toward silicon, optics, security, and AI.

"Regardless of whether individual jobs are being replaced by AI, the money for those roles is," Challenger said in an earlier report this year.

Is AI Washing Inflating the Numbers? What Economists Say

The Challenger methodology records the reason companies themselves report for their cuts — it does not independently verify causation. That distinction has generated sustained scrutiny from economists and, unusually, from some of the technology industry's own leaders.

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman acknowledged the gap in February and again in March, saying: "Almost every company that does layoffs is blaming AI, whether or not it really is about AI." His framing — which he labeled "AI washing" — captures a dynamic Deutsche Bank analysts had predicted in January: "AI redundancy washing will be a significant feature of 2026."

The critics are specific about the mechanism. Marc Andreessen argued that companies are using AI as a "silver bullet excuse" for correcting pandemic-era overhiring — which he estimated left many large tech firms overstaffed by 25% to 75%. Wharton management professor Peter Cappelli put the same point more bluntly: "The headline is, 'It's because of AI,' but if you read what they actually say, they say, 'We expect that AI will cover this work.' Hadn't done it. They're just hoping." Oxford Economics concluded in January that firms "don't appear to be replacing workers with AI on a significant scale."

Even Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang called the practice "lazy," saying it did not make business sense that companies were replacing workers at the current scale through AI systems. Tim Sweeney, CEO of Epic Games, broke with the prevailing corporate script explicitly when announcing more than 1,000 cuts: "Since it's a thing now, I should note that the layoffs aren't related to AI."

EY-Parthenon chief economist Greg Daco offered a more measured reading. "We are seeing a lot of layoff announcements that are supposedly related to greater use of AI," he said. "They are aimed at cutting down on labor expenses, while AI investment is growing very rapidly, but I'm not entirely sure this is a replacement situation where talent is being one-for-one substituted."

What is not in dispute is the budget mechanism. Challenger's Andy Challenger described it plainly: companies are "shifting budgets toward AI investments at the expense of jobs." Not direct replacement — budget reallocation. The distinction matters for workers who are told their roles were automated but whose replacement is a GPU cluster, not a chatbot doing their job.

A Gartner survey of 350 executives at companies with revenue above $1 billion — all of them already deploying AI — found that roughly 80% had reduced headcount. But Gartner analyst Helen Poitevin identified a critical flaw in the premise: "Workforce reductions may create budget room, but they do not create return. Organizations that improve ROI are not those that eliminate the need for people, but those that amplify them." The survey found no meaningful difference in financial returns between companies making the deepest cuts and those cutting the least.

What Happens When AI Absorbs Junior Developer Work

Whatever the precise attribution, the structural effects are measurable and concentrated in a specific cohort. Stanford HAI's 2026 AI Index found that employment for software developers between the ages of 22 and 25 fell nearly 20% from 2024 levels — even as headcount for developers over 26 continued to grow.

The mechanism is architectural. AI tools — code-generation assistants, scripted testing platforms, automated bug-fix pipelines — now perform the boilerplate and routine coding that junior roles were historically built around. Senior engineers, equipped with those tools, can handle the same throughput without a junior team beneath them. The junior role is not being automated directly; it is being compressed out of existence because the work that defined it no longer requires a separate human to execute.

This creates a consequence the data does not yet show but that the talent pipeline structure makes likely: if companies stop hiring junior engineers at scale, they stop producing mid-level and senior engineers at scale. The experience ladder — entry-level to mid-level to principal — runs through the roles that are disappearing. One-third of surveyed organizations in the Stanford report anticipated workforce reductions due to AI in the coming year, with expected cuts highest in software engineering, service operations, and supply chain.

The human cost is already accumulating. A Goldman Sachs analysis published in April 2026 estimated that AI is eliminating roughly 25,000 US jobs per month while creating approximately 9,000 new ones — a net monthly reduction of 16,000. Reemployment timelines for laid-off tech workers are stretching, reflecting both the volume of displaced workers entering the market and a widening mismatch between eliminated roles and available positions.

Ken Matos, an organizational psychologist and director of insights at hiring platform HiBob, expects hiring to rebound after the current investment cycle matures. "Right now, companies are moving labor dollars into tech investment," he said. "Hopefully, that moves back into labor dollars once the technology is set up." But he was direct about who would benefit: "The people who get laid off don't necessarily get the next set of jobs, because the roles are different."

Does the AI Layoff Explanation Hold Up Without a Law to Test It?

One reason the AI washing debate cannot be resolved from the outside is structural: no federal law currently requires employers to disclose whether AI played a material role in a mass layoff.

The federal WARN Act — enacted in 1988, requiring 60 days' advance notice for mass layoffs at companies with 100 or more employees — mandates notice but imposes no obligation to state a reason. A company can publicly attribute thousands of layoffs to AI, and affected workers have no legal right to verify or contest that explanation.

A pending federal bill, the AI Workforce PREPARE Act, would amend the WARN Act to require employers to disclose when AI was a "substantial factor" in a mass layoff, name the AI systems used, estimate the percentage of job losses attributable to them, and describe any retraining steps taken before cuts were made. The bill has not passed.

At the state level, California has moved furthest and fastest. In May, Governor Gavin Newsom signed Executive Order N-6-26, directing the California Labor and Workforce Development Agency to undertake a 180-day review of potential revisions to the state's Cal-WARN Act — including shorter trigger thresholds and new AI-specific disclosure requirements. California also launched what state officials described as the nation's first public dashboard tracking potential AI-related job displacement, using unemployment insurance data to identify early indicators by occupation. Initial data from the tracker showed no evidence of rising statewide unemployment claims in AI-exposed occupations overall, though it did identify impacts on workers in high-exposure positions.

California's SB 951 — which would require 90 days' advance written notice before AI-driven layoffs affecting 25 or more workers — has stalled in the state Assembly. A federal companion bill, the No Robot Bosses Act, which would require human oversight when AI tools are used in employment decisions, remains pending in Congress. Similar disclosure bills have been introduced in at least seven states this legislative session, but none have become law. Colorado's original AI governance law, which had been scheduled to take effect June 30, 2026, was replaced by revised legislation before it took effect; the replacement narrows the state's framework to a disclosure-only model and takes effect January 1, 2027, with enforcement left to the state Attorney General and no private right of action.

RAISE US and the Retraining Gap

On June 25 — eight days before this report — former Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo and former Indiana Governor Eric Holcomb launched RAISE US, a nonpartisan nonprofit backed by Amazon, Anthropic, Microsoft, and the OpenAI Foundation, along with more than two dozen corporate and philanthropic partners. The coalition has raised more than $500 million toward a $1 billion multi-year goal, with initial pilot programs in Arkansas, Connecticut, Maryland, and Utah.

The initiative carries an embedded irony that Raimondo acknowledged directly: the companies building the technology that employers cite when cutting those jobs are writing the first checks to retrain the workers displaced by those cuts. "America has a technology strategy for leading the global AI competition," she said at the launch. "It does not yet have a people strategy, and we cannot lead without one."

Raimondo herself called past federal retraining efforts "ineffective" — a candid assessment that doubles as the central challenge RAISE US must answer. The historical record is not reassuring. A 2025 Brookings Institution analysis reviewed six decades of US federal retraining programs and found that randomized controlled trials showed no statistically significant improvement in employment or earnings for participants. An arXiv study published in May 2026 analyzed more than 23 million participation records from the federal Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act between 2017 and 2023, finding that retraining rarely moved workers into less automation-exposed jobs.

The RAISE US advisory board draws on serious labor economists — MIT's David Autor, Harvard's Raj Chetty, Erik Brynjolfsson — and includes AFL-CIO President Liz Shuler, a deliberate signal of labor-table inclusion. Whether $500 million in privately funded pilots can produce outcomes that six decades of federal programs did not is the question the next three years will answer.

What the Next Four Years Will Reveal

Economists broadly caution against projecting the current trend forward without accounting for the investment cycle it is embedded in. The $700 billion committed by the four largest hyperscalers in 2026 must begin generating sufficient revenue to justify its own depreciation burden — a reckoning that most forecasters place in the 2027-2030 window. If AI-driven productivity gains materialize at the scale companies are betting on, the revenue base expands and some displaced roles eventually return in reconfigured form. If they do not, the workforce reduction will have preceded the productivity gain, leaving both workers and investors worse off.

What is not speculative is the concentration of capital involved. The four hyperscalers are now committing more to data centers annually than ExxonMobil, Chevron, Shell, and BP combined spend on oil and gas exploration. BCG has projected that up to 15% of US jobs could face elimination over the next five years if AI adoption broadens beyond the technology sector — though that scenario, cited in an earlier report, remains unconfirmed at scale and is contested by economists including Daron Acemoğlu, who argues humans will remain necessary and complementary to AI rather than replaced by it.

"Right now, companies are moving labor dollars into tech investment," Matos said. "Hopefully, that moves back into labor dollars once the technology is set up."

For any worker in an AI-exposed role, the question is whether the transition window — during which payroll budgets have shifted to GPU procurement but AI-generated productivity revenue has not yet materialized — is short enough to survive. The H1 2026 Challenger data suggests the window is open and widening. Whether RAISE US, stalled disclosure bills, or eventually a labor market rebound closes it before the next generation of engineers fails to enter the pipeline is what 2026 through 2030 will determine. For workers deciding now whether to stay in an AI-exposed role, upskill toward AI-adjacent work, or wait for the legislative backstop that has not yet arrived, the H1 data offers a clear signal: the restructuring is accelerating, the policy response is thin, and the companies funding the only retraining initiative on the table are the same ones executing the cuts.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are companies really cutting jobs because of AI, or is that a cover story?

Both, according to named economists and company executives. Outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas records whatever reason companies themselves report — no independent verification of causation is required. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman acknowledged in public remarks that some AI attribution is genuine displacement while some is what he called "AI washing": citing AI for cuts the company would have made anyway. Oxford Economics concluded in January 2026 that firms "don't appear to be replacing workers with AI on a significant scale." The clearest documented pattern is budget reallocation: payroll savings from workforce reductions are being redirected to AI infrastructure investment. Challenger's own framing captures this directly — "the money for those roles is" being replaced by AI spending, whether or not the specific task was automated.

Why are profitable companies cutting jobs while spending hundreds of billions on AI?

The financial logic is a capital reallocation story rather than a cost-cutting story. Meta, for example, reported $56.3 billion in first-quarter 2026 revenue — up 33% year over year — while simultaneously executing layoffs and guiding its 2026 capital expenditure to $115-145 billion, mostly for AI infrastructure. The workforce reduction frees up operating cash that is then redirected to infrastructure whose depreciation costs will run for years. Cisco cut 4,000 workers on the day it reported record quarterly revenue of $15.8 billion; its CFO called the restructuring "not savings-driven." A Gartner survey of 350 enterprises found that companies making the deepest cuts showed no improvement in financial returns over those cutting the least — suggesting the layoffs are funding a bet on future AI returns that has not yet materialized.

What federal law protects workers from AI-attributed layoffs, and what are its gaps?

The federal WARN Act (1988) requires 60 days' advance notice before mass layoffs at companies with 100 or more employees — but it does not require employers to state any reason for the layoffs. A company can publicly cite AI as the cause and workers have no legal right to verify or contest that explanation. The pending AI Workforce PREPARE Act would amend WARN to require AI-reason disclosure, including naming the specific systems used and estimating the percentage of cuts attributable to them — but the bill has not passed. California's SB 951, which would require 90 days' notice for AI-driven layoffs, is stalled in the state Assembly. As of now, the federal disclosure gap means that Challenger's AI-cited figures rest entirely on voluntary, unverified corporate self-reporting.

If AI is eliminating junior developer roles, what happens to the next generation of senior engineers?

This is the structural consequence the current numbers do not yet show but the talent pipeline makes likely. Stanford HAI's 2026 AI Index documented a nearly 20% drop in employment for software developers ages 22-25, while headcount for older developers grew. The mechanism is task compression: AI code-generation and testing tools let senior engineers absorb the boilerplate work that junior roles were built around, so companies stop hiring juniors rather than replacing them directly. The consequence is a pipeline gap: if organizations stop training junior engineers at scale in 2026, the cohort that would have become mid-level and senior engineers in 2028-2030 simply does not exist. Gartner forecasts that half of companies that attributed headcount reductions to AI will rehire staff in similar functions under different titles by 2027 — but whether those rehires will come from a junior pipeline that no longer exists is the open question that the H1 2026 data cannot yet answer.

The crypto market is rebounding: BTC needs $67K to reverse course

Market Overview

The crypto market is continuing its recovery, which coincided with the start of the calendar month. Market capitalisation has reached $2.14T, up from a low of $2.02T at the start of 1 July. Buying is taking place across the board. Amongst the individual performers over the last 24 hours, the leaders were Uniswap (+13.1%), SushiSwap (+6.8%) and Cardano (+6.5%), while Hedera (-1.9%) and IOTA (-1.7%) lost ground. The crypto market is edging up from below its 200-week moving average. However, we stand by our view that a dip below, or even an approach to, this line reflects a bearish sentiment in the cryptocurrency market, which often lasts for weeks or months. On the other hand, we have not seen any significant historical falls below this curve, so it would be overly pessimistic, in our view, to expect a pullback to $1T, the starting point of the last bull market in 2023.

Bitcoin rose above $62K at one point on Thursday but retreated to $61.6K amid low trading activity due to the US weekend. On daily timeframes, the RSI has moved out of oversold territory and formed a bullish divergence with the price, indicating that selling momentum is waning and laying the groundwork for a rebound. However, it would only be reasonable to speak of a fundamental reversal of the downtrend if the price consolidates above the $67–68K range, where the local highs from June, the 50-day moving average and the 61.8% retracement level of the May–June decline are concentrated.

News Background

CryptoQuant points to a growing proportion of investors in the red. Losses are reaching record levels, suggesting that the bottom is near.

The collapse in Strategy shares, although painful, is a necessary part of the market cycle, signalling that Bitcoin is approaching its bottom, said Matt Hougan, Chief Investment Officer at Bitwise. In his view, a new BTC bull market could begin this autumn.

Japanese investment firm Metaplanet purchased a further 2,823 bitcoins in the second quarter, according to its quarterly report. The company’s total holdings have reached 43,000 BTC, making it the third-largest corporate holder of the leading cryptocurrency.

According to Arkham Intelligence, brothers Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss transferred Bitcoin and Ethereum totalling around $67 million to hot wallets on their crypto exchange, Gemini. Previously, such transactions were followed by cryptocurrency sales.

Summary: The crypto market is recovering across the board, but for BTC to confirm a reversal, it needs to consolidate above $67–68K. (Media Man Peg On): Just keep enjoying the rollercoast. If it stops being fun, it's time to get out. Or, enjoy the journey!

Eminem loses trademark battle with Aussie beach brand

Swim Shady co-founder Jeremy Scott has issued a statement in which he has welcomed a ruling by trademark authorities against US rapper Eminem. Eminem, whose real name is Marshall Mathers III, had claimed that Swim Shady, a beach brand founded by Scott and partner Elizabeth Afrakoff that began selling beach umbrellas, bags and swimwear in late 2024, had sought to trade off his alternative moniker Slim Shady. In rejecting his bid to essentially block the company from trading under the name Swim Shady in Australia, trademark authorities heard there had been three recent sales of 'Slim Shady' merchandise to customers in Australia. US/World

The dollar: has the trend been broken?

The fall in the USD index could mark the beginning of the end

Rumours of currency interventions sent USDJPY tumbling

The US dollar plummeted to a two-week low amid disappointing labour market figures. Non-farm payrolls rose by 57,000 in June and were revised downwards by 74,000 for April and May. The fall in the unemployment rate to 4.2% was due to a reduction in the labour force rather than an acceleration in hiring.

Against the backdrop of these figures, the probability of a rate rise in July fell from 30% to 20% in the markets; the probability of a rise by September fell from 64% to 53%; and the probability of a rise by the end of the year fell from 83% to 78%. This put pressure on the dollar. Earlier, speculators had increased their net long positions in the US currency to a one-and-a-half-year high, making this trade heavily concentrated. Profit-taking became the catalyst for the rise in EURUSD.

According to Credit Agricole, the US dollar appears overbought and overvalued. The Fed may, in fact, not be as ‘hawkish’ as the markets believe. Eurizon SLJ Capital maintains that investors have already squeezed everything they can out of the USD index rally. The positive sentiment has been fully priced in; it is now time to take profits.

TD Securities argues that as global GDP accelerates and risk premiums narrow, the interest rate differential between the US and the rest of the world will narrow, leading to a weakening of the greenback in the second half of the year. The stabilisation of the international situation following turmoil caused by tariffs and the conflict in the Middle East could also trigger a depreciation of the dollar’s exchange rate.

Meanwhile, the sharpest fall in USDJPY since Japan’s currency interventions in April and May has prompted speculation about whether the authorities have resumed such practices. Was there intervention in the forex market, or did speculators’ fears prompt them to close out the short positions of their own accord?

A Reuters report and a speech by Atsushi Mimura triggered the sell-off in USDJPY. The news agency claims that the government has adopted a new tactic: rather than giving a warning of currency interventions, as it did at the end of April, it will now rely on the element of surprise. The chief currency official, the Vice Minister of Finance for International Affairs, stated that the previous intervention was justified, and that the US does not object to, but rather supports, such actions.

Summary: Weak US economic data has increased pressure on the dollar, whilst rumours of possible intervention by Japan have triggered a sharp fall in USDJPY. (FxPro)

South32 sells aluminium business to Alcoa in $9.8b deal

Alcoa's president and CEO William Oplinger says a deal to acquire South32's alumina assets will generate $US900m worth of synergies. The $US5.6 billion ($8.1 billion) deal includes South32's bauxite mines, smelters and refineries in Western Australia, Brazil and South Africa. Oplinger says the deal is a "great strategic fit" for Alcoa, and he has downplayed fears that it will result in job losses in WA. News

Brent: surplus spooks the bulls

• Goldman Sachs expects an oil market surplus of 3 million BPD in 2027.

• Without Warsh’s help, Japan will be forced to resort to currency interventions.

The Dollar is consolidating ahead of Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra, Portugal, and the release of key US labour market data. A hawkish surprise from the new Fed Chair at the June FOMC meeting sent financial markets into a tailspin, leading to a rise in Treasury yields and the greenback. If the central bank chief hints that he was misunderstood, everything will be turned on its head.

Meanwhile, the strengthening of the dollar has been one of the reasons behind the yen’s fall to 40-year lows and Brent’s rapid return to pre-Middle East conflict levels. In the spring, the rise in oil prices drove the dollar higher, but now the strengthening dollar is putting downward pressure on oil prices. That said, there are already plenty of headwinds for ‘black gold’.

Since the deal with the US was concluded, Iran has exported around 40 million barrels. Coupled with the rise in Russian oil supplies to 4.13M BPD, the highest since the beginning of 2022, this is exerting significant downward pressure on Brent.

Despite flare-ups in the conflict in the Middle East, tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues. The resumption of operations there allows Goldman Sachs to forecast a 3M BPD surplus in the oil market by 2027. Of this, 1 million BPD will be used to replenish strategic reserves. However, the remaining figure will be sufficient to give crude oil bears confidence.

The only thing likely to unsettle them would be the resumption of large-scale US air strikes against Iran. Donald Trump has not ruled out such action, but favours diplomacy.

Meanwhile, the USDJPY has exceeded 162 for the first time since 1986. Investors are bracing for a resumption of currency interventions, on which Japan spent over $70 billion in April and May. According to Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama, the government and the central bank are ready to take appropriate measures regarding currencies at any time as necessary. The official noted that her US counterpart, Scott Bessent, had agreed with this.

If Kevin Warsh’s speech in Sintra fails to halt the US dollar’s advance, Japan will have no choice but to intervene in the forex market.

Summary: A strong dollar and rising supply are weighing on Brent; Japan is preparing to intervene amid a fall in the yen. (FxPro)

July 1

Bitcoin at $59K: Strategy’s sell-off fears are weighing on the market

Market Overview

The crypto market is trading at $2.06T, unchanged from the previous day. Still, the current levels represent a recovery from a low of $2.02T, where the cap had fallen at the start of the active US trading session and again at the opening of the Asian session. Cryptocurrencies remain under pressure from fears of selling by Strategy, casting a shadow over the relatively positive backdrop of favourable stock market dynamics and a declining dollar. Among the top coins over the past 24 hours, the leaders were Stellar (+7%), Cardano (+4.8%) and Bitcoin Cash (+3.6%). The underperformers were Aave (-3.9%), Algorand (-3.2%) and Basic Attention Token (-3%).

The sentiment index plummeted to 11, its lowest level in roughly the last three weeks. June turned out to be a rather gloomy month, as sentiment was above the ‘extreme fear’ threshold of 25 on only the 1st, remaining within the range of 8 to 23 for the rest of the month.

At the start of the day on 1 July, Bitcoin slipped to $57.8K, having recovered to above $59K at the time of writing. At its lowest point, BTC hit its lowest level since September 2024, and the subsequent attempt to rebound still looks rather tentative. The leading cryptocurrency is gradually slipping below the 200-week moving average, which is typical behaviour for it at this point on the curve, judging by several past instances.

Bitcoin fell by 20.5% in June to $58.6K, marking its second consecutive month of decline following a two-month rally. This monthly decline was the steepest in the last four years. From a seasonal perspective, July is generally considered a fairly successful month for BTC. Over the past 15 years, Bitcoin has ended the month higher on ten occasions and lower on five. The average gain was 19%, while the average decline was 7.8%.

News Background

Over the past week, BitMine purchased an additional 27,084 ETH worth $43 million, bringing the total amount of Ethereum in its reserves to 5.7 million ETH, which exceeds 4.7% of the total Ethereum supply. BitMine has achieved 94% of its target to consolidate 5% of the second-largest cryptocurrency's total supply over the past 11 months, according to BitMine's Chairman, Tom Lee.

According to Arkham Intelligence, the US company Sharplink has resumed buying Ethereum after an eight-month hiatus. Last week, the company acquired 39,196 Ether for $62.4 million.

Strategy did not buy any Bitcoin last week, but sold a block of MSTR ordinary shares, increasing its dollar reserves to $2.55 billion. According to the company, these funds will be sufficient to cover 17 months of expected share-based payments.

Following the latest adjustment, the Bitcoin mining difficulty rose sharply by 7.15% to 133.87 T. This is the largest increase in the metric since February.

According to SoSoValue, investors withdrew $4.3B from US Bitcoin ETFs in June. This monthly outflow was the largest since the launch of the Bitcoin ETF in January 2024.

Summary: The crypto market is at lows, with sentiment in the ‘fear’ zone. BTC fell by 20% in June and is trading around $59K, under pressure from selling and ETF outflows, despite a localised recovery. (FxPro)

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

Gold

$4,000: Just a pause, not the end of Gold's decline

Gold is rebounding at the end of the week after a correction in the odds of two key Fed rate hikes this year. Nevertheless, the price is falling for the fourth consecutive week. Since the second half of the week, bears have been relentlessly trying to push the price below the psychologically significant $4,000 mark but eased their grip on Friday afternoon. At the same time, the long-term moving averages favour sellers, as a ‘death cross’ is looming (when the 50-day moving average falls below the 200-day moving average), reinforced by the fact that the price is trading below this cross. On the weekly charts, attempts to push the price back above the 50-week moving average have failed. However, this is also the price support zone from the end of last year, so we can expect a fairly fierce battle around the current level.

It is also worth noting how the narrative for gold’s price movements has changed. Previously, the escalation between the US and Iran triggered downward price movements, with a clear inverse correlation between oil and gold. But it has now become apparent that both assets can move in the same direction – downwards.

However, we must also consider an alternative scenario, in which, since the end of January, the price of gold has been correcting following three years of growth, reaching the 61.8% level—a classic Fibonacci support level. Under this scenario, we should see consolidation followed by a reversal to the upside, with the first stage being a recovery to peak levels around $5,600, and the second stage a rally to $8,000 as part of an extension to the 161.8% level. We nevertheless view this undoubtedly inspiring scenario as less likely, given the history of previous similar price booms and crashes over the last half-century. (FxPro)

Australia Doubles Fines on Social Media Platforms Over Under-16 Ban Failures

The ban, effective December 10, 2025, requires platforms to block under-16 accounts, with companies facing fines up to A$99 million for systemic failures—no penalties for kids or parents. Supporters like NSW Premier Chris Minns praise the tougher stance to protect childhoods, while critics highlight easy workarounds like VPNs and risks of invasive age checks for adults; eSafety is probing Meta, TikTok, Snapchat, and YouTube.

June 30

Oil

Oil finds no silver lining

Oil has returned to the levels seen before the armed conflict in the Middle East much sooner than expected. Brent’s fall of almost 40% from its March highs is not solely due to more barrels coming from the Persian Gulf. The market has adapted to the most far-reaching crisis in the history of the oil market. As a result, traffic through the Strait of Hormuz need not return to previous levels to restore balance. A return to 65%, or 11-12M BPD, will be sufficient.

Morgan Stanley put forward this assumption and lowered its forecast for the average Brent price to $75 per barrel in the third and fourth quarters, a $15 and $5 lower than its previous estimate, respectively. According to the bank, while more barrels are flowing through the main oil artery, the bears’ previous trump cards remain in play. These include record growth in US exports and a decline in Chinese energy imports.

The Gulf states’ search for alternative routes has made it possible to partially meet demand from Europe and Asia for the next couple of months. These continents do not require any more oil. The Iranian output could also surge to 3.3M BPD by the end of the year, following the lifting of sanctions. Combined with the gradual recovery in regional production, this creates a distinctly bearish backdrop for Brent.

There is no doubt that the market will stabilise over the coming weeks. It will need to replenish global stocks, which have been considerably depleted, and meet growing demand from Europe and Asia. Oil prices could even rise if the conflict in the Middle East escalates. If the conflict does not escalate further, the long-term outlook for Brent looks rather bleak.

According to estimates from the International Energy Agency, global crude oil supply in 2026 will fall by 3.9M BPD in 2026. Supply is expected to increase by 8M to 110.3M BPD in 2027. Demand will recover at a much more modest pace, and next year the oil market risks facing a surplus of 5M BPD.

Thus, barring an escalation of the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, the downward trend in Brent is likely to persist.

Summary: Brent has returned to pre-crisis levels, whilst rising supplies and weak demand reinforce bearish sentiment. Barring a new escalation, the decline is likely to continue.

Gold is rebounding at the end of the week after a correction in the odds of two key Fed rate hikes this year. Nevertheless, the price is falling for the fourth consecutive week. On the weekly charts, attempts to push the price back above the 50-week moving average have failed. However, this is also the price support zone from the end of last year, so we can expect a fairly fierce battle around the current level.

It is also worth noting how the narrative for gold’s price movements has changed. Previously, the escalation between the US and Iran triggered downward price movements, with a clear inverse correlation between oil and gold. But it has now become apparent that both assets can move in the same direction – downwards.

However, we must also consider an alternative scenario, in which, since the end of January, the price of gold has been correcting following three years of growth, reaching the 61.8% level—a classic Fibonacci support level. Under this scenario, we should see consolidation followed by a reversal to the upside, with the first stage being a recovery to peak levels around $5,600, and the second stage a rally to $8,000 as part of an extension to the 161.8% level. We nevertheless view this undoubtedly inspiring scenario as less likely, given the history of previous similar price booms and crashes over the last half-century. (FxPro)

押注霍尔木兹航运恢复，机构预测布油年底最低或跌至60美元

财联社7月4日讯（编辑 赵昊）随着全球石油市场有望重新转向供过于求，一些华尔街策略师开始认为布油价格到今年年底可能跌至每桶60美元，这一水平相当于较中东战事期间的峰值腰斩。

花旗集团分析师在周五的一份报告中表示，尽管局势可能出现短暂波动，但美伊双方都有充分理由维持6月中旬签署的谅解备忘录，石油供应增加将在今年下半年对油价形成下行压力。

花旗建议交易员在今夏油价反弹时逢高卖出，并预测到今年年底，布伦特原油价格将在每桶60至65美元之间。

美伊签署谅解备忘录至今已有两周，但双方围绕备忘录的具体条款和执行依然争论不休

对此，花旗分析师写道，“我们预计谅解备忘录将得到维持，并不是因为双方突然建立了信任，而是因为打破协议对双方都缺乏足够的激励。”

“更广泛的政治背景表明，局势将趋于稳定并最终达成协议，而不是重新走向对抗。”

他们还指出，石油市场的供需基本面仍然“结构性疲软”，“如果产油国决定加快已启动的增产计划，市场供需平衡可能迅速转向明显过剩”。

布伦特原油自2月底以来大幅上涨，随着霍尔木兹海峡被迫关闭航运后进一步飙升。4月30日，布油一度突破每桶126美元，创下2022年以来最高水平。

但随着中东冲突缓和，以及6月美伊签署谅解备忘录后，油价持续回落。截至发稿，布伦特原油报每桶72.13美元。

需要指出的是，花旗预测的油价明显低于市场普遍预期。目前，华尔街对2026年底布油价格的预测中值为78美元，而布油2027年1月交割合约报73美元附近。

上月，高盛将其年底布油价格预测下调至每桶80美元。该行近期报告指出，海湾地区石油运输迅速恢复，80美元的预测基于霍尔木兹海峡的航运流量将在7月底前恢复至战前水平。

高盛也表示，在更乐观的供应正常化情景下，布伦特原油价格到2026年底可能降至每桶60美元左右。

不过，这一预测的前提包括：海湾地区石油出口在“7月初”恢复至战前水平、全球原油日产量比预期高出100万桶以上，以及全球需求弱于预期。

花旗分析师称，目前通过霍尔木兹海峡的原油运输量已回升至每日700万桶。与之相比，冲突爆发前约为每日1500万桶。

他们还指出，实际运输量可能高于官方统计数据，因为出于安全考虑，许多油轮关闭了船舶应答器，因此未被完整纳入统计。

7月4日全球财经晨报：流动性投放、成品油下调、贵金属与AI存储链成为今日主线

【美股】

隔夜美股线索相对有限，全球风险偏好更多由欧洲市场、商品价格与国内政策变量共同定价。

欧洲主要股指集体收涨，德国DAX涨1.00%，显示外盘风险偏好阶段性修复。对A股映射看，港股、恒生科技及高弹性成长方向仍需结合流动性与人民币资产预期观察。

【AI存储链】

存储芯片涨价周期继续强化。

瑞银上调存储价格预期，认为2026年下半年DRAM、NAND价格上行空间扩大，DDR合约价格预期三季度环比上涨32%，NAND三季度环比上涨30%，行业供需紧张或延续至2028年上半年。

产业链消息称，三星第三季度DRAM平均售价拟环比提高约20%。上游涨价将向服务器、PC、手机和消费电子传导，短期利好存储链利润弹性，但也会考验下游客户承受能力。

AI数据中心资本开支正在从算力芯片扩散至内存、PCB、液冷、电源与光模块。SemiAnalysis预计，DRAM、NAND和HBM合计在AI服务器系统中的支出占比，到2026年底将超过30%，2027年或超过40%。

A股映射：兆易创新、北京君正、江波龙、佰维存储、深科技、中际旭创、沪电股份。

【日韩股市】

日韩方向今日核心不在指数本身，而在产业链价格信号。

三星DRAM提价预期强化了全球存储周期上行逻辑，后续重点看韩国存储龙头报价、服务器客户订单、HBM供需以及消费电子端能否顺利消化成本。

对A股而言，存储模组、控制芯片、封测、PCB、服务器电源与液冷链条，都可能继续受益于AI资本开支结构扩散。

【贵金属】

贵金属短期震荡，中长期配置逻辑仍被机构看好。

上海黄金交易所黄金T+D夜盘收涨0.41%，白银T+D收涨0.76%；沪金主力夜盘报913元，涨0.81%；沪银主力夜盘报15294元，涨1.61%。

摩根大通预计黄金短期维持区间震荡，但中长期仍偏乐观，预计2026年三季度黄金均价约4300美元/盎司，四季度约4500美元/盎司。

贵金属主线仍由实际利率、储备配置与避险需求共同定价。短线更适合重视交易纪律，避免在单日波动中追涨杀跌。

A股映射：山东黄金、赤峰黄金、中金黄金、银泰黄金、盛达资源。

【原油】

国内成品油迎年内最大降幅。

国家发改委消息，自7月4日起，国内汽、柴油标准品价格每吨分别下调950元、915元。短期看，物流、航空、化工下游成本压力缓释；上游油气链则需继续关注国际油价、库存与投机持仓变化。

市场快照：SC原油主力夜盘报438元，跌0.16%；布伦特原油9月合约结算价72.12美元。

A股映射：中国国航、顺丰控股、中国石化。

【宏观】

央行流动性投放成为今日最高优先级变量。

公开信息显示，央行将于7月6日开展10000亿元逆回购操作，结束连续3个月缩量。短端资金面改善，有助于缓和季初流动性压力，对高股息、券商、银行及估值弹性板块形成情绪支撑。

但持续性仍要看后续资金价格、信用扩张节奏和实体融资需求。

国内服务业仍具韧性。6月RatingDog中国服务业PMI录得54.1，较5月回落0.3个百分点，但连续第42个月处于扩张区间。服务消费、文旅、电影、线下零售等方向仍有结构性支撑。

【国内线索】

证监会就完善上市公司再融资规则公开征求意见，资本市场制度供给继续向规范融资、提高质量、保护投资者倾斜。短期重点看定增、可转债、并购重组与券商投行业务的边际变化。

三部门调整节能汽车、新能源汽车车船税优惠政策，继续强化汽车消费与绿色交通导向。新能源整车、动力电池、热管理、轻量化零部件仍是政策映射方向。

今年2000亿元“两新”设备更新资金已全部下达，叠加循环经济发展规划，政策组合指向制造业设备更新、节能改造、资源循环利用和再生材料体系。

地方住房消费支持工具继续细化：海南允许每年提取一次住房公积金支付物业费，广州南沙商品住房“以旧换新”补贴7月15日启动。

A股映射：中信证券、华泰证券、比亚迪、宁德时代、三一重工、格林美、中国中免、万达电影、万科A、保利发展。

【科技产业】

英飞凌全球最大功率半导体工厂提前投产，投资额达50亿欧元。新能源车、光伏储能、工业电源与AI数据中心电力系统升级，继续推动功率半导体景气度修复。

A股映射：士兰微、华润微。

有色金属方面，钴出口配额与行政流程扰动提高短期供应不确定性。市场估算最多约2万吨钴存在无法按期发运风险，涉及价值约11亿美元。若现货报价与库存继续共振，钴资源、回收链和三元材料可能出现事件型交易机会。

A股映射：华友钴业、寒锐钴业、格林美、洛阳钼业。

【今日主线】

第一，央行万亿逆回购改善资金面预期，高股息、银行、券商与估值弹性资产受到关注。

第二，国内成品油大幅下调，航空、物流、化工下游成本压力缓释。

第三，钴供应扰动带来有色金属短线弹性，但需警惕流程恢复后的溢价回吐。

第四，黄金和白银中长期逻辑仍在，但短线更适合波段纪律。

第五，存储芯片涨价周期与AI数据中心内存支出占比抬升，是今日科技产业最强主线。

7月6日：央行计划开展10000亿元逆回购操作。

Five regions. Offline simultaneously.

🇷🇺 RUSSIA — 25–50% of national refining capacity DARK. 8 of 10 largest refineries struck. Moscow Kapotnya: offline until 2027. KINEF: fully down since May 5. Diesel seaborne exports collapsed to 426 kb/d — down 50% YoY, lowest since Jan 2017. Gasoline export ban in force.

🛢️ PERSIAN GULF — $58B in damage. 84 facilities struck. ~400–600 kb/d still offline. Kuwait’s three refineries impaired. Bapco (Bahrain): force majeure. UAE Fujairah: hit four times. Pearl GTL (140 kb/d): down. Ras Tanura the only major restart. IEA: 2-year recovery runway.

🇲🇽 MEXICO — TWO refineries down. Combined design capacity: ~665 kb/d. Salina Cruz (325 kb/d): Diesel hydrotreaters and reformer offline since May 12 — the specific units producing the product most needed right now. Refinería Olmeca, Dos Bocas (340 kb/d): Cogeneration electrical failure June 24. Full emergency shutdown. Smoke column visible for miles. Third major incident at this plant in four months. No credible restart timeline on either facility.

🇺🇸 US EAST COAST — Monroe Energy (Trainer, PA) dark since June 25. No restart timeline. Delta’s 185 kb/d refinery — supplying ~75% of the airline’s total jet fuel via pipeline to NY and Atlanta — taken out by a pump room fire 6 days ago. Delta’s CFO had projected a $300M Q2 benefit from the plant. That hedge just burned. East Coast distillate output hit a 2026 low of 208 kb/d the same week, down 10% WoW. Stocks scraping 23-year lows.

🇨🇳 CHINA — ~3 mb/d offline. Teapot utilization: 72% → 54%. Iranian crude flows down 57%. Teapot volume loss alone exceeds most countries’ entire refining systems

⚠️ THE DIESEL CRISIS IS HERE — AND JET FUEL’S LIFELINE IS FRAYING

Russia was the world’s 2nd largest diesel exporter — 11% of global seaborne supply. Russian seaborne diesel exports collapsed to 426 kb/d in June, half of where they were a year ago. June 29: Putin floated a diesel export ban. Backed off. Threat still live. US distillate stocks near 23-year lows. NW Europe diesel stocks down ~20%. The Gulf’s product machine is 2 years from recovery. There is no buffer.

On jet: Asia-Pac has stabilized — but only through demand destruction. Airlines are cutting flights, not filling tanks. Europe is holding via a fragile US export lifeline that has surged from 22 kb/d to 107 kb/d since Hormuz closed — a 5x increase that is now the single pillar keeping European aviation fuel stocks above critical lows. Kpler puts European jet stocks drawing toward 50–55 Mbbls, with a hard floor of 30–35 Mbbls. The margin for error is razor thin.

Monroe Energy going dark June 25 threatens that lifeline at exactly the wrong moment — US summer driving season is already pulling refinery yields toward gasoline and away from jet. Every barrel the US can’t export to Europe is a barrel Europe can’t replace.

One export ban announcement away from a structural diesel crisis. One bad week for US jet exports away from tipping Europe’s aviation fuel market.
